query: association between vertical lines in distal esophageal mucosa and esophagitis in children

retrieved_docs.title: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Esophagus
retrieved_docs.content: The esophagus is located posterior to the trachea and begins distal to the cricoid cartilage and ends at the cardiac orifice of the stomach. It ranges in diameter from 4 to 6 mm and in length from 9 to 10 cm in the term infant to approximately 25 cm in the adult. The change in the mucosa color from pale- to reddish-pink marks the transition from the esophagus and gastric epithelium (Z line).

retrieved_docs.title: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy -- Technique or Treatment -- Esophagus and Esophagogastric junction
retrieved_docs.content: After intubating the esophagus, the scope is advanced down the esophagus lumen while simultaneously examining the mucosa for any inflammation, ulcerations, furrowing, varices, narrowing or strictures. The location of the esophagogastric junction should be noted. The squamocolumnar junction, also referred as Z-line, is the area where the squamous epithelial lining of the esophagus (pale pink colored) meets the columnar lining mucosa of the stomach (salmon-colored). The level of the Z-line should also be noted. If the Z-line is displaced proximal to the gastroesophageal junction, biopsies should be taken to evaluate for Barrett esophagus.

retrieved_docs.title: Plummer-Vinson Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The esophageal web is investigated using radiographic tools, such as a barium swallow, which is generally readily available, even in remote locations, and serves as a diagnostic tool with the advantage of reproducible documentation. Videofluoroscopy is generally more reliable for demonstrating esophageal webs. [29] A dynamic x-ray investigation evaluates swallowing as the barium bolus moves to the esophagus from the mouth. An advantage of these newer techniques is the identification of minor webs and the distinguishing between true and false webs. Fiberoptic endoscopy is the safest and most reliable tool for GI tract examination. Esophagoscopy, an endoscopic examination of the esophagus, can be both diagnostic and therapeutic in the same setting. During esophagoscopy, esophageal webs appear as smooth, thin, gray lesions with normal-appearing mucosa and a central or lateral lumen and are most commonly located on the anterior wall of the esophagus.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophagitis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The endoscopic appearance of the mucosal lesions can help with diagnosis. In patients with suspected eosinophilic esophagitis, endoscopy may reveal white exudates or papules, red furrows, corrugated concentric rings, and strictures; but endoscopy may be normal in up to 10% of patients. Endoscopic signs of candidiasis are small, diffuse, linear, yellow-white "cheese-like" plaques adherent to the mucosa. CMV esophagitis is characterized by several large, shallow, superficial ulcerations. HSV esophagitis results in multiple small, deep ulcerations.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Esophagus -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The esophageal wall consists of four layers: a mucosa, a submucosa, a muscularis propria, and an adventitia. The mucosa is the innermost layer that lines the esophageal lumen. It starts with a mucous membrane comprised of stratified squamous epithelium similar to that of skin. A thin layer of connective tissue called the lamina propria follows the mucous membrane. Finally, a muscularis mucosa made primarily of irregularly arranged smooth muscles forms the deepest part of the mucosa. The next layer lying beneath the mucosa is the submucosa, comprised predominantly of blood vessels, small mucous glands, and connective tissue. It also contains a nerve plexus called the submucosal or Meissner's plexus. Underneath the submucosa lies the muscularis propria, which is more complicated than the previous layers. The muscularis propria, also called the muscularis externa, includes an inner circumferential and an outer longitudinal layer, which can be variably composed of striated or smooth muscle. Striated muscles predominate in the proximal esophagus, while smooth muscles predominate in the distal esophagus. A transition zone consisting of both muscle types lies in between the proximal and distal ends. The muscular layer also contains an additional nerve plexus called the myenteric or Auerbach's plexus. The final outermost layer is the adventitia, which is an external fibrous layer that connects the esophagus to other external structures. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Ulcer -- Evaluation -- Endoscopic Findings
retrieved_docs.content: In esophageal Crohn disease, ulcers and erosions are surrounded by normal-appearing mucosa. Parts of the mucosa may exhibit cobblestoning. The middle and distal esophagus are most commonly involved. Ulcers secondary to Behcet disease are found in the middle-to-lower third of the esophagus and appear as small oval, discrete ulcers with erythematous margins. These lesions may also occur as longitudinal, elliptical ulcers with irregular borders. Endoscopic findings in caustic injuries are scored using the Zargar classification as follows: Grade 0: Normal mucosa Grade I: Edema and erythema of the mucosa Grade IIA: Superficial ulcerations, erosions, blisters, friability, exudates, or hemorrhages Grade IIB: Circumferential ulcerations Grade IIIA: Focal areas of necrosis, seen as deep gray or brownish-black ulcers Grade IIIB: Extensive areas of necrosis Grade IV: Perforation [45]

retrieved_docs.title: Plummer-Vinson Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Other causes of dysphagia are much more common than Plummer-Vinson syndrome; even the presence of iron deficiency anemia or an esophageal web should not lead to a diagnosis of PVS. Tracheoesophageal fistula in neonates, injuries to the esophagus, like blunt trauma or penetrating injuries, gastroesophageal reflux disease, vascular rings, and diverticula are some of the conditions that can present with similar dysphagia and spectrum of symptoms. Motility disorders like scleroderma, achalasia, and diffuse esophageal spasm may present with an esophageal web and dysphagia. Benign and malignant tumors of the esophagus can have a similar presentation. Other conditions to consider in the differentials of PVS include the following: Heterotopic gastric mucosa with stricture [21] [22] Pill-induced stricture Hypopharyngeal bar Inflammatory conditions associated with webs, such as chronic graft versus host disease, blistering skin diseases

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Trauma -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The esophagus is a muscular conduit, approximately 25 cm long, extending from the lower end of cricoid to the stomach. In the neck, the esophagus originates at the level of the sixth cervical vertebral body. The esophagus then descends anterior to the vertebral column through the superior and posterior mediastinum. It terminates at the cardia, corresponding to the level of 11th-12th thoracic vertebrae. The esophagus has four layers: the mucosa, submucosa, muscularis propria, and adventitia. The muscularis propria is critical to esophageal structure and function. It is composed of two layers of muscle fibers: The inner (or circular) layer and the outer (or longitudinal) layer.

retrieved_docs.title: Lymphocytic Esophagitis -- Evaluation -- Esophagogastroduodenoscopy
retrieved_docs.content: The gross endoscopic features of LE vary widely. They may range from a normal-appearing esophagus to findings of esophageal strictures or rings, linear furrows, erosive esophagitis, esophageal edema, or white plaques. [3] [10] With conventional endoscopy evaluation, as many as 23% to 55% of patients with histologic LE will have normal esophageal mucosa. [1] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Ulcer -- Evaluation -- Alternative Imaging Modalities
retrieved_docs.content: Ulcers related to GERD are typically seen in the lower third of the esophagus on radiographs and appear as linear, stellate, or punctate lesions surrounded by mucosal edema, which presents as a radiolucent halo on barium swallow studies. HSV esophagitis appears as multiple small, circular, stellate, or punctate ulcers surrounded by edema. CMV ulcers can vary in number and appearance, often oval or diamond-shaped and can be several centimeters long, surrounded by mucosal edema.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Webs and Rings -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal webs are usually found in the proximal esophagus and have a covering of squamous epithelium. [11] Esophageal rings, on the other hand, are usually located in the distal esophagus and can be covered with squamous epithelium, columnar epithelium, or both depending on their location to the squamocolumnar junction: The A rings are located proximal to the squamocolumnar junction. They are covered entirely with squamous epithelium. They are uncommon and usually nonpathological. The B rings or Schatzki rings are located precisely at the squamocolumnar junction. The proximal part is covered with squamous epithelium, and the distal portion is covered with columnar epithelium. They are the most common type of ring and can become symptomatic. The C rings are located distal to the squamocolumnar junction. They are covered entirely with columnar epithelium. They are the least common, rarely symptomatic, and usually represent diaphragmatic indentation of the esophagus. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Webs and Rings -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review features of conditions frequently associated with esophageal webs or rings. Explain how a patient with an esophageal web or ring might present. Describe the treatment of esophageal webs and rings. Summarize interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to enhance outcomes for patients affected by esophageal webs and rings. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Eosinophilic Esophagitis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopic findings of EoE include corrugated mucosa, longitudinal mucosal furrows, fixed esophageal rings or trachealization, whitish mucosal plaque or exudate, stricture, superficial mucosa tear upon passing endoscope, diffusely narrow lumen, and mucosal friability giving the appearance of crepe paper. Clinicians also should note that some patients may have normal esophagus in upper endoscopy. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Perforation and Tears -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The esophagus, a 25 cm-long fibromuscular tube, extends from the pharynx to the stomach, beginning at the level of the C6 vertebra in the neck and descending through the mediastinum before passing through the diaphragm at the T10 vertebra. This vertical structure includes 3 anatomical constrictions that are significant in the context of esophageal perforations—the cricopharyngeal sphincter, located approximately 15 cm from the upper incisor teeth; the crossing of the aortic arch and the left main bronchus at about 23 cm; and the lower esophageal sphincter at 40 cm, where the esophagus passes through the diaphragm.

retrieved_docs.title: Caustic Ingestions -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical constriction: located 15 cm from the incisors and formed by the cricopharyngeus muscle; also known as the upper esophageal sphincter (UES) Broncho-aortic constriction: a compound constriction; the aortic arch crosses the esophagus 22.5 cm from the incisors, while the left main bronchus crosses the tube 27.5 cm from the incisors Diaphragmatic constriction: located 40 cm from the incisors; the area passing through the esophageal hiatus of the diaphragm, also known as the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) These constrictions are important landmarks during esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) and radiologic evaluation of esophageal lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Webs and Rings -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The esophagus is a muscular tube for the propulsion of food from the pharynx to the stomach. Histologically, the esophageal wall consists of mucosa, submucosa, and muscularis propria. [1] Esophageal webs and rings are indentations of the esophageal wall that may partially occlude the esophageal lumen. Esophageal webs and rings are usually asymptomatic but can occasionally present with intermittent dysphagia to solids. Esophageal webs are classically associated with Plummer-Vinson syndrome (PVS). It includes the triad of iron-deficiency anemia, postcricoid dysphagia, and upper esophageal webs. Dysphagia is painless and slowly evolving, starting with solid foods and difficulty swallowing liquids after years of onset. Dysphagia becomes symptomatic only when the luminal diameter in the region of the esophageal web becomes less than 12 mm. Esophageal webs and rings are usually asymptomatic but can occasionally present with intermittent dysphagia to the solids. Dysphagia in PVS is generally graded I (occasional dysphagia on taking solids) or grade II (able to swallow only semi-solid diet). [2]

Endoscopic dilation of the esophagus and endoscopic pneumatic dilation are both forms of esophageal dilatation that belong to the category Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy with biopsy (mapped from upper gastrointestinal endoscopy; biopsy) and are classified as therapeutic esophageal procedures. Esophagoscopy for control of hemorrhage and esophagoscopy for injection of esophageal varices are procedures on the esophagus (inverse_isa) grouped with other non‑OR upper GI therapeutic procedures. Esophageal varices (دوالي المَريء) are described morphologically as a dilated vein (وريد متدلي) and are a central concept linking several vascular conditions: they co‑occur with intestinal varices (دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة), are located at the site of esophageal stenting (مفاغرة مريئية), and are the target of operations such as esophageal variceal ligation (ربط دوالي المريء) and variceal injection. retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopic dilation of the esophagus is a specific form of esophageal dilatation, representing a child procedure within the broader category of esophageal dilatation techniques.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2710
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopic pneumatic dilation of the esophagus is a specific type of esophageal dilatation, representing a child procedure within the broader category of esophageal dilatation techniques used to widen a narrowed esophagus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 1109
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopic dilation of esophagus and Endoscopic pneumatic dilation of esophagus are both procedures that originate from the broader activity of upper gastrointestinal endoscopy with biopsy, indicating that they are performed in the context of an endoscopic examination of the upper GI tract that includes tissue sampling. Both of these dilation techniques are specific forms of esophageal dilatation, positioning them as child procedures within the esophageal dilatation category. Esophageal dilatation itself is organized under the higher‑level grouping of “الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالمريء” (therapeutic procedures of the esophagus). This therapeutic esophageal category also encompasses other interventions such as “ربط دوالي المريء” (esophageal variceal ligation) and “مفاغرة مريئية” (esophageal stenting), which are listed as its child procedures. Additionally, “Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy with biopsy” is classified as a type of “upper gastrointestinal endoscopy; biopsy,” reinforcing its role as a diagnostic and therapeutic endoscopic procedure. Together, these relationships illustrate how various endoscopic and dilatation techniques are hierarchically organized within the spectrum of esophageal therapeutic interventions, aiding clinicians in selecting appropriate procedures for managing esophageal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2923
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal dilatation is a therapeutic category that includes both Endoscopic dilation of esophagus and Endoscopic pneumatic dilation of esophagus as specific child procedures. These endoscopic techniques are employed to widen a narrowed esophagus, with the pneumatic method using controlled air pressure to achieve dilation. Both procedures fall under the broader concept of esophageal dilatation and are utilized in the management of esophageal strictures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2593
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopic dilation of esophagus and Endoscopic pneumatic dilation of esophagus are specific therapeutic techniques used to widen the esophagus. Both procedures are mapped from the broader category upper gastrointestinal endoscopy; biopsy, indicating that they are performed in the context of an upper gastrointestinal endoscopic examination that may include tissue sampling. In the procedural hierarchy each of these techniques is classified as a child of the general category esophageal dilatation, reflecting that they are specialized forms of esophageal dilatation. Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy with biopsy classifies the category upper gastrointestinal endoscopy; biopsy, linking the diagnostic endoscopic approach to the therapeutic dilatation procedures. Together, these relationships demonstrate how esophageal dilatation interventions are integrated within diagnostic upper gastrointestinal endoscopy workflows.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 596
retrieved_docs.content: Correction of esophageal atresia includes the procedure عملية في المريء. Esophagoscopy for control of hemorrhage is classified as a procedure on the esophagus (inverse_isa) and is related to other non‑oral upper gastrointestinal therapeutic procedures. Esophagoscopy for injection of esophageal varices is directly linked to دوالي المَريء (esophageal varices) and is described by the indirect morphology وريد متدلي, a term also used to characterize the morphology of Operation on esophageal varices, ربط دوالي المريء (ligation of esophageal varices), and the procedures varicose vein stripping; lower limb and varicose veins of lower extremity. Gastrointestinal hemorrhage commonly manifests as دوالي المَريء and is associated with نزيف الدوالي الوريدية (bleeding of varicose veins), which in turn is a subtype of وريد متدلي. The location of دوالي المَريء is مفاغرة مريئية, and مفاغرة مريئية is listed as a child of الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالمريء (esophageal therapeutic procedures), alongside ربط دوالي المريء. The Structure of vein of lower extremity serves as the finding site for varicose veins of lower extremity and as the direct procedure site for varicose vein stripping; lower limb. Together, these relationships illustrate how esophageal interventions, variceal pathology, gastrointestinal bleeding, and lower‑extremity varicose‑vein procedures are interconnected through shared morphological descriptors and procedural classifications, informing comprehensive patient assessment and coordinated therapeutic planning.

These techniques are mapped from “upper gastrointestinal endoscopy; biopsy,” indicating that they are performed in the context of an upper GI endoscopic examination that may include tissue sampling. retrieved_docs.content: Correction of esophageal atresia is linked to the procedure termed عملية في المريء, which serves as a child operation within that corrective approach. Both Endoscopic dilation of esophagus and Endoscopic pneumatic dilation of esophagus are forms of esophageal dilatation; they are mapped from the category upper gastrointestinal endoscopy; biopsy and each has a child relationship to the broader esophageal dilatation concept. The procedure Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy with biopsy classifies the category upper gastrointestinal endoscopy; biopsy, reinforcing its role as a diagnostic and therapeutic tool. Esophageal dilatation itself is categorized under the parent procedure عملية في المريء, indicating that all esophageal dilatation techniques, including the endoscopic and pneumatic variants, are considered sub‑procedures of this overarching esophageal operation. Together, these relationships illustrate how corrective surgery for esophageal atresia, diagnostic endoscopy with biopsy, and various dilatation techniques are organized within a hierarchical framework of esophageal interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 180
retrieved_docs.content: on the esophagus and classified under both “الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالمريء” and “عملية في المريء”. Child procedures within this group are esophageal variceal ligation (ربط دوالي المريء) and esophageal stenting (مفاغرة مريئية). Upper gastrointestinal x‑ray imaging is commonly used to evaluate the esophagus for upper GI bleeding, which is closely linked to variceal bleeding (نزيف الدوالي الوريدية). Understanding these interconnected anatomical sites, procedural categories, morphological features (dilated veins), and clinical associations (alcohol use, liver disease, variceal hemorrhage) guides clinicians in selecting appropriate endoscopic, surgical, or radiologic interventions and in anticipating related gastrointestinal complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 180
retrieved_docs.content: Correction of esophageal atresia (عملية في المريء) is performed as an esophageal operation and is linked to the broader category of esophageal procedures. Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy serves as the diagnostic and therapeutic foundation for esophageal interventions; its children include endoscopic dilation of esophagus, endoscopic pneumatic dilation of esophagus, and upper gastrointestinal endoscopy with biopsy, all classified under the procedure group esophageal dilatation. Esophagoscopy for control of hemorrhage and esophagoscopy for injection of esophageal varices (دوالي المَريء) are therapeutic esophageal procedures that target dilated submucosal veins (وريد متدلي). Gastrointestinal hemorrhage frequently manifests as esophageal variceal bleeding and is associated with upper gastrointestinal bleeding (نَزِيف الجزء العلوي من الجهاز الهضمي); the varices co‑occur with intestinal varices (دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة) and are located in the esophageal region of stenting (مفاغرة مريئية).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2592
retrieved_docs.content: Correction of esophageal atresia and operation on esophageal varices are both categorized as عملية في المريء (esophageal procedures). The correction of esophageal atresia addresses a congenital discontinuity of the esophagus by surgically restoring continuity, whereas the operation on esophageal varices treats dilated esophageal veins that are prone to bleeding. Grouping these distinct interventions under the broader term عملية في المريء highlights their shared focus on surgical management of esophageal pathology and aids clinicians in organizing appropriate treatment strategies for patients with esophageal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2711
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopic dilation of esophagus is linked to the procedural category upper gastrointestinal endoscopy; biopsy, indicating that it is considered an alternative or related mapping of that endoscopic examination. Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy with biopsy is classified under the same category of upper gastrointestinal endoscopy; biopsy, reflecting its role as a diagnostic approach that includes tissue sampling. Together, these statements illustrate that both the therapeutic Endoscopic dilation of esophagus and the diagnostic Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy with biopsy belong to the broader domain of upper gastrointestinal endoscopic procedures, highlighting their complementary roles in evaluating and managing esophageal pathology.
